topic: Someone finds out their medical records were mixed up with another patient's
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the hospital waiting room seemed to intensify, casting a harsh glare on my face. I clutched the manila folder so tightly my knuckles were white. Inside, the diagnoses, the test results, the intimate details of my health—they were all wrong. The date of birth was theirs, the medications listed were completely foreign, and the surgical notes detailed procedures I had never undergone. A cold sweat prickled my hairline. I felt a sudden, dizzying wave of nausea, and I quickly retreated to a quiet corner, trying to regulate the rapid thumping in my chest. When the nurse finally called my name, my voice came out thin and reedy. "I need to speak with Dr. Evans immediately," I managed, my gaze fixed firmly on the scuffed linoleum floor, acutely aware of every eye that might have glanced in my direction.

[story 2]

I stared at the printed summary, the ink blurring slightly under the pressure of my thumb. It was an absolute disaster. My name was listed next to a completely different set of symptoms—a chronic condition I didn't have, a history of allergies that weren't mine. A wave of intense heat washed over my cheeks, and I instinctively pulled my cardigan tighter around myself, as if trying to physically shield myself from scrutiny. Every muscle in my jaw felt tight, locked in a silent grimace. When the administrative assistant approached, I kept my hands deliberately still, forcing my breathing to remain even, though I could feel the frantic flutter beneath my ribs. "There's been an error," I stated, the words sounding strained and overly formal. "These records... they don't belong to me."

[story 3]

The discovery hit me like a physical blow. I reread the file three times, each time the realization growing heavier, sinking into my core. The sheer absurdity of the situation made my stomach churn violently. I stood up too quickly, nearly knocking over a nearby chair, and I felt a desperate urge to simply disappear. My lips pressed into a thin, white line, and I avoided looking up, focusing instead on the intricate pattern of the carpet. When the doctor finally entered the room, I offered a small, jerky nod, my entire posture radiating a profound sense of awkwardness and profound embarrassment. "This is... a significant mix-up," I whispered, the sound barely audible above the hum of the ventilation system.
